title: Interview Opening at the Highlander Center
summary: An opening verse of "Ain't You Got a Right to the Tree of Life" gives way to Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program introducing the September 19, 2011 joint interview with Candie and Guy Carawan at the Highlander Center in New Market, Tennessee, recorded with videographer John Bishop for the Civil Rights History Project.

title: The April 1960 Highlander Sit-In Gathering
summary: Candie recalls Highlander hosting the April 1960 gathering of sit-in students at Monteagle, Tennessee, where she arrived with the Nashville delegation. Guy taught freedom songs including "I'm Gonna Sit at the Welcome Table," "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize," and "We Shall Overcome" that the Nashville students lacked.

title: The Highlander Questioning Process
summary: Candie describes Myles Horton and others using the Highlander process of hard questions to push students' thinking, with breakout sessions on whether non-violence was tactic or philosophy and on the jail-no-bail strategy. Guy kept the singing going with James Bevel and Bernard Lafayette when sessions grew heavy.

title: Driving Septima Clark Around the South
summary: Guy recalls being Septima Clark's driver in the early Highlander days, including police stops because Clark, a Black woman, sat in the front seat beside a younger white man. Candie remembers Clark mentoring Guy on his dress and conduct.

title: Septima Clark, Tough and Sensible
summary: Candie remembers Clark as a sensible neighbor in Charleston who advised letting their son keep his toy guns, and insists Clark was a tough woman who had to be strong working atop Monteagle in an all-white community, contradicting her popular image as merely sweet.

title: Myles Horton the Educational Visionary
summary: Candie and Guy characterize Myles Horton as an educational visionary whose method built on people's own experience, down to earth and never intimidating yet unwilling to let foolish statements pass. They credit his vision to the civil rights and labor movements while noting Highlander was always a team that included women.

title: A California Upbringing Toward Fairness
summary: Candie traces her motivation to a progressive California family, a Unitarian fellowship, and the Girl Scouts, all stressing fairness and equality. The Montgomery bus boycott on the new family television set and high-school study of Gandhi awakened her sense that change in the South might be possible.

title: Pomona College and the Fisk Exchange
summary: Candie describes choosing Pomona College partly for the Fisk Exchange Program, gaining a roommate through an earlier exchange, and applying in her junior year. She arrived in Nashville in January 1960, lucky to land in the crucial sit-in semester between January and June.

title: Preparing in Nashville Before Greensboro
summary: Asked about an NAACP sense that Greensboro was coming, Candie recalls non-violent trainings under Reverend James Lawson at Fisk University, the feeling of preparing for something undefined, and the electric reaction when the Greensboro sit-in began and showed the Nashville students what to do.

title: Adapting Church Songs Into Freedom Songs
summary: Candie recalls early Nashville singing drawing on rock and roll, camp songs, and church hymns, including the forerunner "Amen" reworked toward civil rights justice. Meeting Guy brought the practice of taking religious songs and changing a few words to make freedom songs, alongside the powerful community singing of the Nashville mass meetings.

title: Guy's Musical Roots in Los Angeles
summary: Candie recounts Guy's formation: people-songs work in Los Angeles, the Oliver family who connected him to political musicians like Pete Seeger and the labor-music tradition, folklore study at Occidental, and a sociology master's at UCLA where he absorbed Library of Congress recordings and the Lomaxes' work on Southern music.

title: Wayland Hand's Warning and Seeger's Tip
summary: Candie tells how UCLA folklorist Wayland D. Hand warned Guy to keep folk music politically neutral, citing Nazi use of folk music, advice Guy ignored. After meeting Pete Seeger and dreaming of the banjo, Guy followed Seeger's tip to visit Highlander Folk School, touring the South in summer 1953 with Rambling Jack Elliott and Frank Hamilton.

title: Carrying Songs Into Southern Churches
summary: Married in 1961, the Carawans traveled to civil rights communities where Guy taught and learned songs in Black churches, welcomed as a novelty though barred from playing banjo inside. Candie describes joining him and the warmth of communities opening their churches and homes.

title: Jailed After the Clarksdale Concert
summary: Candie recounts a Clarksdale, Mississippi joint concert arranged by Mrs. Pigee pairing Guy's folk songs with opera student Mary Jane Pigee. Community members escorted them out of the dangerous Black part of town, but police followed and took them to jail while Candie was heavily pregnant.

title: Singing Keep Your Eyes on the Prize
summary: Guy fetches a second guitar and the Carawans perform verses of "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize" and "We Shall Overcome," including the line about the truth making us free.
keywords: Keep Your Eyes on the Prize

title: Jamila Jones and We Are Not Afraid
summary: Candie identifies Jamila Jones, a thirteen-year-old with an adult Montgomery group, as the person who hummed and inserted the "We Are Not Afraid" verse during the 1959 raid on Highlander, in the dark with police inside the building.

title: Encouraging Song Leaders and Workshops
summary: Candie stresses that Guy's lasting contribution was nurturing music and song leaders, helping take the Freedom Singers to the Newport Folk Festival, and organizing five or six song-leader workshops with Myles Horton's backing in Atlanta, Mississippi, and at Highlander.

title: From Song Leader to Field Recorder
summary: Candie describes Guy's 1961 realization at the Jackson release of the Freedom Riders, who emerged from Parchman already overflowing with songs, that his role should shift to documentation. He bought a big Ampex tape recorder and began recording movement songs across the South, knowing Moe Asch's Folkways could distribute them nationally.

title: Folkways, Sing Out, and the First Songbook
summary: Candie and Guy credit Moe Asch of Folkways Records and Irwin Silber of Sing Out! magazine for circulating freedom songs, with Oak Publications issuing the first songbook of movement music. She notes broadside carried Northern folk-revival writers like Tom Paxton, Phil Ochs, and Bob Dylan more than Southern material.

title: Singing Paul and Silas, Hold On
summary: Asked again about Nashville mass-meeting community music, Guy responds with a performance of "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize / Hold On," the Paul and Silas verse.
keywords: Keep Your Eyes on the Prize

title: Building a Beloved Community Through Song
summary: Candie reflects on the movement's hope of building a beloved community, contrasting it with present cynicism. She quotes John Lewis that a movement without its music is a bird without wings, and notes Guy's point that there were movement songs for every mood, jubilant, sorrowful, and humorous.

title: Bernice Reagon on Music as Force
summary: Candie recalls working with Bernice Johnson Reagon on the We Shall Overcome Fund, which returns commercial royalties to Southern communities, and relays Reagon's account of movement singing as an aggressive, powerful non-violent tool that drew her into the Albany Movement and now carries into other struggles.

title: Satirical Songs and We'll Never Turn Back
summary: Candie offers examples of the satirical Nashville Quartet songs and recalls that when "We Shall Overcome" came to feel too optimistic and overused, people switched to the harder-edged "We'll Never Turn Back," noting Bernard Lafayette's gift for the comic numbers.

title: Confronting Fear in the Movement
summary: Asked how she handled chronic fear, Candie says her own fear was nothing next to that of Black Southerners who organized despite far greater danger, and reflects that being among courageous colleagues let her overcome fear at the time, even as she carries it more now, having raised a child in the movement between 1962 and 1965.

title: Singing Tree of Life From Johns Island
summary: The Carawans perform "Ain't You Got a Right to the Tree of Life," a song collected on Johns Island, South Carolina, with its verses on planting, struggle, and friends tried and true.

title: The Story of Ed Cabbell
summary: Candie tells the story of Ed Cabbell, a West Virginia African American friend and historian who insists on the long, strong history of Black people in Appalachia, recalling his surprise at the mixed singing group when he first came to a Highlander workshop before meeting Guy.

title: When White Activists Were Challenged in 1965
summary: Candie describes the 1965 moment when white people were voted out of SNCC amid growing cynicism about racism, and credits Myles Horton for taking it seriously, affirming that Black people led the civil rights movement and that white organizers should build allies and work where racism lived in white communities.

title: Highlander Turns Toward Appalachia
summary: Candie explains how Septima Clark moved to SCLC's Citizenship Education Program with Bernice Robinson following, and how Highlander brought Appalachian staff aboard in the mid-1960s, sending them into mountain communities to find places with both anger and hope where people might be moved to act.

title: Leaving Johns Island for New York
summary: Candie recalls the couple's years on Johns Island, South Carolina working with Bill Saunders and older tradition-bearing musicians, their self-questioning about being so immersed in a Black community while putting on festivals of its music, and their move to Pittsfield, New York for two years to publish collected materials and revive Guy's singing career.

title: A Highlander Stint in Pike County
summary: While living in Pittsfield, the Carawans got a call from Highlander to join the team listening to Appalachian communities, and spent about six months in Pike County, Kentucky, a sharp contrast and a major learning experience after Pittsfield and the Black South.

title: Recruited to Pitzer College
summary: Candie recounts a call from a political scientist named Lucien at the new, experimental Pitzer College in Claremont, California, inviting Guy to teach. With their son Evan ready for kindergarten and their next move uncertain, the family relocated to Claremont.

title: The Appalachian Field Study Program
summary: Candie tells how their son found kindergarten dull after a life of moving, and how Guy, after one semester, created an Appalachian Field Study Program taking Pitzer College and Pomona College students into the mountains. The interview closes with Candie performing Joan Boyd's "Come Home," a song from Big Stone Gap, Virginia, and the LoC and Smithsonian closing card.
